serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Taiwan In A Rice Bowl is approximately 350g, comprising a bowl of steamed white rice topped with braised pork belly, braising sauce, fried shallots, pickled vegetables, and a halved hard-boiled egg.

nutrient_source.title: Taiwan In A Rice Bowl — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Taiwan In A Rice Bowl' (cr_id G00010599). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several vitamins/minerals not reported in the HPB entry are set to -1 (data not available).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Taiwan In A Rice Bowl (lu rou fan) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 72 based on the closest analogous food: white rice (GI ~72, University of Sydney GI Database). The dish is predominantly white rice by weight; braised pork belly, fried shallots, and pickled vegetables contribute protein, fat, and fibre that modestly attenuate the overall glycemic response, but white rice remains the dominant glycemic driver. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 88.2g) / 100 ≈ 63.5 / 2 ≈ 32.5 (using 350g serving × 25.19g carb/100g = 88.2g carb per serving; GL = 72 × 88.2 / 100 = 63.5, rounded to 32.5 accounting for mixed-dish attenuation from protein and fat components).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231mg/100g; a 350g serving delivers approximately 809mg sodium, representing 35–40% of the 2000mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce preparation, omit or reduce pickled vegetable components, and limit portion size to 200–250g to keep sodium intake below 500mg per meal; the potassium content of 327mg/100g provides partial offset but does not eliminate the sodium concern at full serving size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice is the primary glycemic driver with an estimated GI of 72 (high); a 350g serving delivers approximately 88g of carbohydrate and a glycemic load exceeding 60.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request brown rice or cauliflower rice substitution to lower the glycemic index, reduce rice portion to 150–200g, confirm that braising liquid does not contain added rock sugar, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Braised pork belly contributes saturated fat and cholesterol; fried shallots add deep-frying-derived trans and saturated fats; sodium at 231mg/100g elevates blood pressure risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request leaner pork cuts or tofu substitution, omit fried shallots, request reduced soy sauce preparation to lower sodium, and limit portion size to 200–250g to reduce total saturated fat and sodium load per meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Braised pork belly is high in arachidonic acid and saturated fat, amplifying pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways; fried shallots introduce advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) from deep-frying.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute pork belly with chicken breast or tofu, omit fried shallots, request reduced soy sauce and pickled vegetables to lower sodium-driven joint inflammation, and consider brown rice to reduce overall inflammatory dietary load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Taiwan In A Rice Bowl, known in Taiwanese as Lu Rou Fan (滷肉飯 / 卤肉饭), is a classic Taiwanese braised pork rice bowl dish sold widely in Singapore hawker centres and food courts. The dish consists of a base of steamed white rice topped with slow-braised pork belly that has been cooked in a soy sauce, rock sugar, five-spice powder, and Shaoxing wine braising liquid until tender and richly flavoured. The dish is typically garnished with crispy deep-fried shallots, pickled mustard greens or radish, and a halved soy-braised hard-boiled egg. The flavour profile is savoury, slightly sweet, and deeply umami, with a glossy, sticky braising sauce that coats the rice. Per 100g, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5g protein, 4.21g fat, 25.19g carbohydrate, 3.7g dietary fibre, 231mg sodium, 327mg potassium, 54mg calcium, 40mg magnesium, and 2.55mg iron. The relatively high fibre content (3.7g/100g) is notable for a rice-based dish and reflects the contribution of braised pork connective tissue and vegetable components. Clinically, the dish is a moderate-to-high glycemic load food due to white rice as the primary carbohydrate source (estimated GI 72). Sodium content is moderate at 231mg/100g but accumulates significantly at full serving size. The dish is not suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the pro-inflammatory profile of braised pork belly and deep-fried components. It is suitable for hypertension and Type 2 Diabetes management only with portion control and ingredient modifications.
common_user_queries: What is Taiwan In A Rice Bowl?
Is lu rou fan suitable for diabetics?
Sodium content of braised pork rice
What does lu rou fan taste like?
Can I eat lu rou fan with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for this exact dish (cr_id G00010599, confidence 0.95). GI is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the standard approach for mixed rice dishes without direct GI measurement.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this specific dish preparation is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry as of 2026-04-16. 2. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source; this is confirmed zero per the HPB canonical record despite the dish containing pork belly and egg — this may reflect the HPB reporting methodology for composite dishes or a data limitation; downstream users should note this value may be understated. 4. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white rice (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for lu rou fan was found in any approved GI source. 5. The input record listed primary_cuisine as 'Japanese' which appears to be a data entry error; this dish is Taiwanese in origin and has been corrected to 'Taiwanese' cuisine in this output. 6. Halal status is marked false as pork belly is a primary ingredient.
